summary: Gruber argues against Christopher Mims's WSJ claim that native apps are killing the open web. He contends that native apps are simply superior client interfaces built on top of HTTP, the same protocol that powers the web — they represent a new layer, not a replacement. He dismantles Mims's claim that Apple takes 30% of all app transactions by pointing out that retailers like Amazon pay Apple nothing for physical goods sold through their apps. Gruber further argues that the browser wars of the 1990s were far more hostile to openness than today's native app ecosystem, and that the iPhone era has produced a renaissance of innovation, not a decline. He concludes that if you define 'the web' as all traffic over HTTP/S rather than just what renders in a browser, native apps have made the open web stronger.
key_points: Native apps are alternative clients to HTML browsers, not opponents of the open web — they still use HTTP/S for networking and sit on top of the same infrastructure
Apple's 30% cut applies only to in-app digital content purchases, not to physical goods or services sold through apps — a distinction Mims and his sources get wrong
The browser wars of the 1990s were far more hostile to web openness than today's native app ecosystem, contradicting the nostalgia for browser-era compatibility
Facebook's ability to lock content within its platform has nothing to do with native apps specifically — it could do exactly the same thing through a web-only interface
The mobile app era from 2007-2014 has been a renaissance of innovation and user-created content, not the decline Mims claims
money_quotes: Users love apps, developers love apps — the only people who don't love apps are pundits who don't understand that apps aren't really in opposition to the open Internet.
They're just superior clients to open Internet services.

It's just a conceptual simplification. Instead of a web app running inside a browser running as an app inside an OS (three levels of abstraction), we just have apps running within an OS (two levels). Simpler, easier, more elegant.
Retailers who sell through native apps do not pay Apple anything, let alone 30 percent.
If something works great as a web app, let it be a web app. If something works better as a native app, let it be a native app.
The weird thing about a truly open platform is that its openness allows closed things to be built on top of it.
Far from killing it, native apps have made the open web even stronger.
key_insight: Native apps aren't killing the web — they're a better client layer built on top of HTTP, and expanding the definition of 'the web' beyond the browser reveals that apps have made the open web stronger, not weaker.
